request.question: The actor who starred in "Three Socks" and "Hustle" also starred in a miniseries with Anglea Bassett that is set in what city?

title: Josh Peck
Jeremy Sisto
Robert Glenister
Close to the Enemy
Matt Di Angelo
David Huynh
Laura Marano
Bassett Creek (Crystal, Minnesota)
Low Winter Sun (U.S. TV series)
Riz Ahmed
sentences: Joshua Michael Peck (born November 10, 1986) is an American actor, voice actor, comedian and YouTube personality.
He is known for playing Josh Nichols in the Nickelodeon live-action sitcom "Drake & Josh".
He began his career as a child actor in the late 90s and early 2000s, and became known to young audiences after his role on "The Amanda Show".
He has since acted in films such as "Mean Creek", "Drillbit Taylor", "The Wackness", "ATM", and "Red Dawn", along with voicing Eddie in the "Ice Age" franchise.
He also starred as Gerald in a lead role with John Stamos in the series "Grandfathered".
He currently voices Casey Jones in "Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles".
He also starred in a Netflix comedy, "Take the 10", along with Tony Revolori.
Jeremy Merton Sisto (born October 6, 1974) is an American actor, producer, and writer.
Sisto has had recurring roles as Billy Chenowith on the HBO series "Six Feet Under" (2001–05), and as NYPD Detective Cyrus Lupo on NBC's drama series "Law & Order" (2008–10).
He also starred in the comedy "Clueless" (1995), the biblical miniseries "Jesus" (1999), the drama "Thirteen" (2003), and the horror film "Wrong Turn" (2003).
In 2004, he starred as bigoted baseball player Shane Mungitt in "Take Me Out", for which he was nominated for a Los Angeles Drama Critics Circle Award for Featured Performance in a Play.
In 2006, he starred in the play "Festen" on Broadway.

Robert Lewis Glenister (born 11th March 1960) is an English actor known for his roles as con man Ash "Three Socks" Morgan in the BBC television series "Hustle" and Nicholas Blake in the BBC spy drama "Spooks".
Close to the Enemy is a British period drama miniseries set in the late 1940s in London.

It is written and directed by Stephen Poliakoff, starring Jim Sturgess, Freddie Highmore, Charlotte Riley, Phoebe Fox, Alfred Molina, Lindsay Duncan, August Diehl, Alfie Allen, Angela Bassett, Antje Traue, Lily G and Robert Glenister.
It premiered in the United Kingdom on BBC Two on 10 November 2016.
Mario Angelo Constantinou (born 1 May 1987), professionally known as Matt Di Angelo, is a British actor and singer, best known for his role as Dean Wicks in the BBC soap opera "EastEnders".
He also starred as Sean Kennedy in the BBC drama series "Hustle".
David Huynh (born February 5, 1983) is a Canadian actor.

Huynh won a Special Jury Prize Award at the 2007 Los Angeles Asian Pacific Film Festival for Outstanding Newcomer and Best Emerging Actor for his performance in Juwan Chung's "Baby", which also won a Special Jury Award for Best Feature Length Film at that year's Festival.
Huynh has also Starred in Byron Q's film, "Bang Bang" (2011), which won a Special Jury Award for Best First Feature, Narrative, at the 2011 Los Angeles Asian Pacific Film Festival.
He also played Jonathan Chen in Byron Q's film, "Las Vegas Story" and Ty Do in Mark Tran's film "All About Dad".
He recently stars in a Web Series entitled "Hollywood Aliens" from Beyond Cinema Productions, directed by "Bang Bang" Director Byron Q and Kevin Boston.

He also appears as the protagonist "Fong" in a pilot entitled "Chinatown Squad" about 1890s San Francisco Chinatown, directed and produced by Stephane Gauger and written by and starring "Baby" co-star Feodor Chin, who plays the antagonist, "Pistol Pete."

He has also starred as Benson Fong in Timothy Tau's short film bio-pic Keye Luke, which premiered at the 2012 Los Angeles Asian Pacific Film Festival and which was Closing Night Film of the inaugural 2013 Seattle Asian American Film Festival.
Laura Marie Marano (born November 29, 1995) is an American actress and singer.
She starred in the Disney Channel series "Austin & Ally" as Ally Dawson.
Marano was one of the five original classmates in "Are You Smarter than a 5th Grader?
She starred in "Without a Trace" for three seasons and also "Back to You", in both instances playing the daughter of the main characters.
Marano starred in the indie film "A Sort of Homecoming".
Marano also starred in the 2015 Disney Channel Original Movie "Bad Hair Day" along with "Good Luck Charlie" actress Leigh-Allyn Baker.
In 2015, she signed with Big Machine Records and released her debut single "Boombox" on March 11, 2016.
At the end of 2016, Big Machine Records made the decision to drop all of their pop artists.
Marano then signed to Warner Bros.
Records in 2017 and plans to release her debut album with the label.
She is also the younger sister of actress Vanessa Marano who starred in Freeform's "Switched at Birth".
Bassett Creek is a neighborhood within the city of Crystal, Minnesota.

It borders the city of Minneapolis to the south and east, and is generally considered to be the section of Crystal that is situated east of Douglas Drive, south of 36th Ave. N., and west of Highway 100.
Bassett Creek is bordered by Crystal neighborhoods Lee Park to the east, Valley Place to the west, and Fair to the north.
The neighborhood is the location for Bassett Creek Park, the largest park in Crystal.
The park has a 3,700 sq. ft. playground, a baseball field, softball field, hockey rink, four volleyball courts, three tennis courts, 12 holes of disc golf and 1.06 miles of asphalt trails.
A neighborhood association has been formed to assist in the upkeep of the park facilities.
The neighborhood is also part of the Bassett Creek Watershed, and is the location of the Bassett Park Pond sub-watershed.
The watershed is overseen by the Bassett Creek Watershed Management Commission.
Low Winter Sun is an American crime drama television series that aired on AMC from August 11 to October 6, 2013, for one season consisting of ten episodes.
The series was developed by Chris Mundy and starred Mark Strong and Lennie James.
It is based upon the 2006 British two-part miniseries of the same name which also starred Strong in the same role.
Filmed and set in Detroit, Michigan, the series follows detectives Frank Agnew and Joe Geddes after they murder a corrupt cop and attempt to cover it up, and explores organized crime in Detroit.
The series received generally mixed reviews and AMC announced in December 2013 that it had canceled the series.
Rizwan Ahmed (Urdu: ‎ ; born 1 December 1982), also known as Riz MC, is a British-Pakistani actor, rapper and activist.
As an actor, he won an Emmy Award, out of two Emmy nominations, and was also nominated for a Golden Globe, Screen Actors Guild Award, and three British Independent Film Awards.

He was initially known for his work in independent films such as "The Road to Guantanamo" (2006), "Shifty" (2008), "Four Lions" (2010), "Trishna" (2011), and "Ill Manors" (2012), before his breakout role in "Nightcrawler" (2014).
In 2016, he starred in "Una", "Jason Bourne", and as Bodhi Rook in the first "Star Wars" "Anthology" film, "Rogue One".
That year, he also starred in the HBO miniseries "The Night Of" as Nasir Khan; the show and his performance were critically lauded.

At the 2017 Emmy Awards, he received two nominations, for his performance in "The Night Of" and his guest spot in "Girls"; he won the award for Outstanding Lead Actor in a Limited Series or Movie for "The Night Of", becoming the first Asian and first Muslim to win in the category, the first South Asian male to win an acting Emmy, and the first Muslim and first South Asian to win a lead acting Emmy.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: The actor who starred in "Three Socks" and "Hustle" also starred in a miniseries with Anglea Bassett that is set in what city?
hop1_docs: The Chef, the Actor, the Scoundrel | The Chef, the Actor, the Scoundrel is a 2013 Chinese comedy film directed by Guan Hu, starring Liu Ye, Zhang Hanyu and Huang Bo.  The film is meant to be "mass entertainment", in the words of Guan, who also co-wrote the screenplay.  The film is set in 1942, during the Second Sino-Japanese War.  Much of the plot revolves around the fates of two Japanese scientists who are captured in Beiping (Beijing) by the three title characters, who have trouble deciding what to do with them.

Robert Glenister | Robert Lewis Glenister (born 11th March 1960) is an English actor known for his roles as con man Ash "Three Socks" Morgan in the BBC television series "Hustle" and Nicholas Blake in the BBC spy drama "Spooks".

Alessandro Nivola | Alessandro Antine Nivola (born June 28, 1972) is an American actor and producer, who has starred in feature films such as "Face/Off", "American Hustle", "A Most Violent Year", and also a brief appearance in "Selma".

My Body, My Child | My Body, My Child is a 1982 television film directed by Marvin J. Chomsky and starring Vanessa Redgrave.  It premiered on ABC on 12 April 1982.  It includes early performances by future "Sex and the City" co-stars, Sarah Jessica Parker and Cynthia Nixon.  It is also the final television role of Jack Albertson, who was subsequently nominated posthumously for the Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Supporting Actor in a Miniseries or a Movie.

Close to the Enemy | Close to the Enemy is a British period drama miniseries set in the late 1940s in London.  It is written and directed by Stephen Poliakoff, starring Jim Sturgess, Freddie Highmore, Charlotte Riley, Phoebe Fox, Alfred Molina, Lindsay Duncan, August Diehl, Alfie Allen, Angela Bassett, Antje Traue, Lily G and Robert Glenister.  It premiered in the United Kingdom on BBC Two on 10 November 2016.

Science of Star Wars (miniseries) | Science of Star Wars is a 2005 TV documentary miniseries aired by the Discovery Channel.  The series features mainly archive footage from the actors of the original and prequel trilogies of the "Star Wars" saga.

The Legendary Ranger | The Legendary Ranger (, Yuan Zhen-xia) is a 1993 Hong Kong cross-genre television miniseries produced by Mui Siu-ching and aired by TVB.  The series starred Leon Lai, Michelle Reis and Catherine Hung, and featured a supporting cast of veteran actors, stars, and future stars, including Faye Wong and Athena Chu.  It is based on Ni Kuang's fictional surgeon/adventurer Yuan Zhen-xia and is set in the Wisely fictional universe, a setting in which extraterrestrial aliens, magic and wuxia heroes co-exist.
hop1_titles: The Chef, the Actor, the Scoundrel
response.summary_1:
Evidence found:
- Robert Glenister — Passage 2: "Robert Lewis Glenister (born 11th March 1960)"
- "Three Socks" (role) — Passage 2: "Ash \"Three Socks\" Morgan"
- "Hustle" (series) — Passage 2: "the BBC television series \"Hustle\""
- Close to the Enemy (miniseries) — Passage 5: "Angela Bassett, Antje Traue, Lily G and Robert Glenister"
- Setting of the miniseries — Passage 5: "set in the late 1940s in London"

Ambiguities:
- "Anglea Bassett" (user query) is a misspelling; passages show "Angela Bassett".
- "Hustle" could mean the BBC TV series or films with similar titles; Passage 2 specifies the BBC television series "Hustle", resolving the intended work here.

Bridge / missing fact:
- All required relations are present and unambiguous: the actor (Robert Glenister) who played "Three Socks" in "Hustle" also appears with Angela Bassett in Close to the Enemy, which is explicitly set in London. No additional bridge facts needed.

Retrieval direction:
- Open page "Close to the Enemy" to verify setting and cast.
- Open page "Robert Glenister" to verify roles "Ash 'Three Socks' Morgan" and appearance in Close to the Enemy.
- Open page "Angela Bassett" for cast confirmation.
Final conclusion: The miniseries is set in London.
hop2_docs: Close to the Enemy | Close to the Enemy is a British period drama miniseries set in the late 1940s in London.  It is written and directed by Stephen Poliakoff, starring Jim Sturgess, Freddie Highmore, Charlotte Riley, Phoebe Fox, Alfred Molina, Lindsay Duncan, August Diehl, Alfie Allen, Angela Bassett, Antje Traue, Lily G and Robert Glenister.  It premiered in the United Kingdom on BBC Two on 10 November 2016.

Keep Your Enemies Close | Keep Your Enemies Close is a British children's game show that tests best friends by wrenching them apart and forcing them to keep their enemies close and work with their rivals instead.  To get ahead, the friends must compete alongside their enemies and tackle challenges in the name of friendship.

The Final Enemy | The Final Enemy is an adventure module for the "Advanced Dungeons & Dragons" (AD&D) fantasy roleplaying game written by Dave Browne with Don Turnbull set in the "World of Greyhawk" campaign setting.

Closed set | In geometry, topology, and related branches of mathematics, a closed set is a set whose complement is an open set.  In a topological space, a closed set can be defined as a set which contains all its limit points.  In a complete metric space, a closed set is a set which is closed under the limit operation.

Trench knife | A trench knife is a combat knife designed to kill or gravely incapacitate an enemy soldier at close quarters, as might be encountered in a trenchline or other confined area.  It was developed in response to a need for a close combat weapon for soldiers conducting assaults and raids on enemy trenchlines during the First World War.  An example of a World War I trench knife is the German Army's "Nahkampfmesser" (close combat knife).

Final protective fire | Final protective fire refers to a tactical plan for a military unit, set up by the forward observer in support of the infantry, to protect itself against overwhelming attack.  A final protective fire is an immediately available preplanned barrier of direct and indirect fire designed to provide close protection to friendly positions and installations by impeding enemy movement into defensive areas.

Near sets | In mathematics, near sets are either spatially close or descriptively close.  Spatially close sets have nonempty intersection.  In other words, spatially close sets are not disjoint sets, since they always have at least one element in common.  Descriptively close sets contain elements that have matching descriptions.  Such sets can be either disjoint or non-disjoint sets.  Spatially near sets are also descriptively near sets.
response.summary_2: Robert Glenister appears in the miniseries Close to the Enemy, which is set in the late 1940s in London.
